docs.title: HFrEF Medications
docs.text:
Mortality-reducing meds (the GDMT four)

  1. ACEi/ARB (or ARNI — Sacubitril/Valsartan)
  2. Beta-blocker (Carvedilol, Metoprolol succinate, Bisoprolol)
  3. MRA (Spironolactone, Eplerenone)
  4. SGLT2i (Dapagliflozin, Empagliflozin)

docs.title: Acid-Base Abnormalities
docs.text: Last updated: Mar 21, 2026

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text:
Pearls

Learn the compensation equations: the body will not over compensate — it is an additional underlying process.

docs.title: Clinical Evaluation of Metabolic Acidosis
docs.text:
1. Determine Anion Gap
  2. Determine Winter's (underlying additional respiratory derangement)
  3. Determine Delta-Delta (ΔAG / ΔBicarb)

(Anion Gap − 12) / (Bicarb − 24)

Result Interpretation ΔAG ≈ ΔBicarb No other process. Isolated pure anion gap metabolic acidosis. ΔAG >> ΔBicarb Second process increasing bicarbonate → AGMA + Metabolic Alkalosis ΔAG << ΔBicarb Second process decreasing bicarbonate → AGMA + NAGMA

NAGMA → consider ordering urine electrolytes (Na, K, Cl)

Urine Anion Gap = (UNa + UK) − UCl

  • Positive gap (>1): RTA
  • Negative gap (<1): GI loss, IV Fluid

docs.title: Winter's Formulas
docs.text:
Bootleg Winter's

pCO₂ should ≈ last 2 digits of pH if respiratory compensation is adequate.

  • e.g. pH 7.15 → pCO₂ should be ~15
  • If pCO₂ is higher → suspect additional respiratory acidosis
  • If pCO₂ is lower → suspect additional respiratory alkalosis

Simplified Winter's

Predicted PaCO₂ = HCO₃⁻ + 15

Works for PaCO₂ between 10–40.

Real Winter's

Predicted PaCO₂ = 1.5 × HCO₃⁻ + 8 (±2)

docs.title: Determining Prior Bicarb
docs.text:
HCO₃(before) = HCO₃(now) + (AG(now) − AG(normal))

Can help identify underlying metabolic derangements prior to the current acute disorder.

docs.title: Bicarbonate Buffer System (Le Chatelier's Principle)
docs.text:
CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻

  • Metabolic acidosis (↑ H⁺) → left shift → ↑ CO₂ → compensatory hyperventilation
  • Metabolic alkalosis (↑ HCO₃⁻) → right shift → ↓ CO₂ → compensatory hypoventilation + urine bicarb excretion

pH = balance of CO₂, H⁺, and HCO₃⁻

Mediator Primary Organ CO₂ Lungs (ventilation) HCO₃⁻ Kidneys (urine) H⁺ GI (stomach acid) and kidneys (urine)

docs.title: Acids and Bases in Blood
docs.text:
When a Brønsted-Lowry acid (e.g., lactic acid, ketoacids) is added to plasma, it dissociates into H⁺ and its conjugate base (an anion, such as lactate⁻ or β-hydroxybutyrate⁻). The H⁺ is buffered primarily by HCO₃⁻, which is consumed in the process → decrease in serum bicarbonate. The conjugate base remains in serum, increasing unmeasured anions → raises the calculated anion gap ([Na⁺] – ([Cl⁻] + [HCO₃⁻])).

The decrease in serum bicarbonate is directly matched by the accumulation of unmeasured anions, maintaining electroneutrality.

docs.title: Compensation
docs.text:
When the underlying process (respiratory or metabolic) drives pH outside the normal range, the alternate system "compensates."

  • Metabolic acidosis → compensatory respiratory alkalosis

Lung Compensatory Mechanisms:

  • Hyperventilation → expel CO₂ → ↑ pH (compensatory respiratory alkalosis)
  • Hypoventilation → retain CO₂ → ↓ pH (compensatory respiratory acidosis)

Kidney Compensatory Mechanisms:

  • Respiratory acidosis → retain HCO₃⁻ → ↑ pH (compensatory metabolic alkalosis)
  • Respiratory alkalosis → excrete HCO₃⁻ → ↓ pH (compensatory metabolic acidosis)

Anion Gap Pearl

The mean anion gap is 8–12 mEq/L (SD ±2 mEq/L). An anion gap of 20 is ~4–5 SD above the mean — 99.999% chance of AGMA. No need to wait for blood gases to diagnose.

docs.title: Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis (AGMA)
docs.text:
AGMA occurs when accumulation of acids leads to ↓ pH via ↑ H⁺ and ↑ unmeasured serum anions (A⁻).

Weak Acid: HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻

Simple DDx: KULTS

Letter Cause K Ketones U Uremia L Lactate T Toxic ingestion (toxic alcohols, APAP, etc.) S Salicylates

Osmolar Gap Screening

AGMA → measure serum Osm → calculate Osm gap. Easily screens for any alcohol intoxication.

Common Osmolar Gap AGMA Culprits:

Substance Source Methanol Windshield wiper fluid, paint, paint thinners, wood stains Ethylene glycol Antifreeze, engine coolants, industrial solvents Propylene glycol Diluent in parenteral meds: diazepam, lorazepam, phenobarbital, phenytoin, nitroglycerin Diethylene glycol Industrial solvent Isopropyl alcohol Hand sanitizer, rubbing alcohol Ethanol Good ol' fashioned alcohol

Full DDx: GOLDMARK

Letter Cause Mechanism G Glycols (ethanol, ethylene glycol) Consume Krebs cycle intermediaries → organic acid buildup O Oxoproline (Acetaminophen) Glutathione depletion → pyroglutamic acid buildup L L-Lactic acidosis Type A (hypoperfusion), Type B (catecholamine/medication driven) D D-Lactic acidosis Abnormal bacterial fermentation in GI tract (rare; short bowel syndrome) M Methanol Consumes Krebs enzymes → formic acid buildup A Aspirin (Salicylates) Disrupts Krebs cycle, decouples mitochondria → organic acids + lactic acid; also directly stimulates respiratory centers (typically AGMA + resp. alkalosis) R Renal failure (Uremia) GFR <15 → retention of phosphites, sulfites, nitrites K Ketoacidosis (DKA) Lactic acid + ketones

MUDPILES adds:

  • Iron — multifactorial; fluid loss, cardiogenic shock, hepatic failure → lactic acidosis
  • Isoniazid — inhibits lactate→pyruvate conversion via vitamin B6 depletion

Osmolar Gap Clinical Presentations:

Substance Products Clinical Features Methanol Formic acid AMS, blurry vision, dilated pupils, papilledema Ethylene glycol Oxalic acid AMS, CN palsies, flank pain, hematuria, hypocalcemia with tetany, calcium oxalate crystals (envelope-shaped), AKI Propylene glycol Lactic acid AKI, liver injury Diethylene glycol Diglycolic acid AKI, N/V, hepatitis, pancreatitis, neuropathy, AMS, elevated lactate Isopropyl alcohol Acetone AMS, fruity breath, pancreatitis, elevated lactate with normal/mildly elevated AG Ethanol Acetaldehyde Ketoacidosis, lactic acidosis, metabolic alkalosis (vomiting)

Lactate Reference:

Level Significance 0.5–2 mM Normal 2–4 mM Mild/moderately elevated >4 mM Unequivocally elevated — concerning for occult shock or serious illness

docs.title: Non-Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis (NAGMA)
docs.text:
Caused by GI or renal loss of HCO₃⁻ (removal of base rather than addition of acid). Results in equivalent accumulation of serum chloride.

Simple DDx: HARD

Letter Cause Mechanism H Hyperchloremia Normal saline boluses → ↑ urinary chloride → forces urinary HCO₃⁻ excretion (beta-intercalated cell exchanges HCO₃⁻ for Cl⁻) A Acetazolamide Inhibits HCO₃⁻ reabsorption in the proximal tubule R Renal Tubular Acidosis Kidney excretes excess HCO₃⁻ when it shouldn't — can be genetic or acquired (e.g., tacrolimus) D Diarrhea GI loss of bicarb (pancreas!)

Full DDx — Non-RTA causes:

  • NaCl, KCl, CaCl₂ administration
  • Plasma exchange with 4% albumin
  • TPN with inadequate acetate
  • Diarrhea (especially secretory), high-output fistulas, pancreatic/biliary drainage
  • Cholestyramine or sevelamer hydrochloride
  • Moderate renal insufficiency (GFR ~20–40 mL/min — inadequate ammoniagenesis)
  • Resolving DKA
  • Ureteroileostomy or ureterosigmoidostomy

docs.title: Low Anion Gap (<8)
docs.text:
Causes:

Increased Chloride:

  • Hypertriglyceridemia
  • Bromide
  • Iodide

Decreased Unmeasured Anions:

  • ↓ Albumin
  • ↓ Phosphorus

Increased Unmeasured Cations:

  • Hyperkalemia
  • Hypercalcemia
  • Hypermagnesemia
  • Lithium
  • Cationic paraproteins (IgG, etc.)

docs.title: Metabolic Alkalosis
docs.text:
Most commonly caused by loss of chloride → retention of bicarbonate. The kidney retains HCO₃⁻ to maintain serum electroneutrality (partly via beta-intercalated cells).

Three Basic Mechanisms:

Mechanism Example How Urine loss Loop diuretics Inhibit Na/K/2Cl transporter in ascending Loop of Henle → massive Cl⁻ loss → kidney retains HCO₃⁻ GI loss Vomiting Loss of Cl⁻ from gastric juices (alkaline tide) Oral alkalinization Antacid overconsumption Patient ate an entire bottle of Tums

Myth

Contraction alkalosis is a lie that we have been told and have been passing down to future generations for far too long.

DDx:

Saline Responsive (hypochloremia):

  • True volume depletion
  • NG suction / nausea / vomiting
  • Diuretic use

Saline Refractory:

  • Hypokalemia
  • Milk-Alkali Syndrome
  • Mineralocorticoid excess states
  • Bartter Syndrome
  • Gitelman Syndrome

docs.title: Respiratory Acidosis
docs.text:
Info

Respiratory acidosis is caused by hypoventilation. Period.

Hypoventilation has many causes (pneumonia, excess sedation, pneumothorax, etc.) but the clinical presentation will usually reveal those details.

docs.title: Respiratory Alkalosis
docs.text:
Info

Respiratory alkalosis is caused by hyperventilation. Period.

Most commonly caused by anxiety or stress. Some pathologies can also drive hyperventilation (e.g., salicylate toxicity).

docs.title: Notes in this section
docs.text: • Antibiotics Overview — Full reference with all classes

docs.title: Coverage at a Glance
docs.text: Gram+ ONLY Vancomycin, Linezolid, Daptomycin, Clindamycin Gram− ONLY Aztreonam MRSA Vancomycin, Linezolid, Daptomycin, Ceftaroline, TMP-SMX Pseudomonas Pip/Tazo, Ceftazidime, Cefepime, Meropenem, Ciprofloxacin Anaerobes Metronidazole (below), Clindamycin (above), Pip/Tazo, Carbapenems VRE Linezolid, Daptomycin, Ampicillin

docs.title: Antibiotics Overview
docs.text: Last updated: 2026 · ~50 min read

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text:
Gram+ ONLY Vancomycin, Linezolid, Daptomycin, Clindamycin Gram− ONLY Aztreonam Anaerobe coverage Clindamycin (above diaphragm), Metronidazole (below), Beta-lac/BLI combos, Moxifloxacin, Meropenem Broad G+/G− 3rd/4th gen cephalosporins, Beta-lac/BLI combos, Carbapenems, Moxifloxacin MRSA coverage Vancomycin, Linezolid, Daptomycin, Ceftaroline, Clindamycin, Tetracyclines, TMP-SMX Pseudomonas Pip/Tazo, Ceftazidime, Cefepime, Aztreonam, Cipro ± , Meropenem, Imipenem VRE Daptomycin, Linezolid, Ampicillin

Clinical Pearl

If a GNR (esp. E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus) is resistant to ceftriaxone → suspect ESBL. Avoid all beta-lactams.

docs.title: I. Beta-Lactams
docs.text:
Classes: PCNs, Cephalosporins, Carbapenems, Monobactam (Aztreonam)

Mechanism: Bind PBPs in cell membrane → inhibit cell wall crosslinking → bactericidal

Side effects: Hypersensitivity/anaphylaxis, rashes, bone marrow suppression, interstitial nephritis, GI (C. diff), seizures (high dose + renal failure)

Key rule

No beta-lactam covers MRSA (except Ceftaroline) or atypical intracellular organisms (Legionella, Mycoplasma, Chlamydia).

docs.title: Penicillin Allergy
docs.text: Situation Action Reported PCN allergy ~85–90% will actually tolerate PCN Cross-reactivity with cephalosporins ~2% (in those with positive skin test) Cross-reactivity with carbapenems <1% PCN + Aztreonam No cross-reactivity (except Aztreonam ↔ Ceftazidime — identical side chain)

docs.title: A. Penicillins
docs.text:
1. Penicillin G (IV) / V (PO)

  • Spectrum: Streptococci, minority of Staph, some Enterococcus, oral anaerobes, Syphilis
  • DOC for Group A Strep (universally PCN-sensitive)
  • Used for: Strep throat, Syphilis, bacteremia/endocarditis from susceptible organisms

2. Aminopenicillins — Ampicillin (IV), Amoxicillin (PO)

  • Spectrum: Strep, Enterococcus, Listeria — NOT MSSA; limited GN (no Klebsiella, Moraxella, SPICE-A)
  • DOC for Enterococcal infections if susceptible (E. faecalis usually yes, E. faecium usually not)
  • Amoxicillin = best-absorbed beta-lactam (75–90% bioavailability)

3. Anti-Staphylococcal PCNs — Nafcillin/Oxacillin (IV), Dicloxacillin (PO)

  • Spectrum: MSSA, Strep — no MRSA
  • DOC for MSSA infections
  • For serious MSSA infections (bacteremia, osteomyelitis, endocarditis) → must be IV for entire course

4. Pip/Tazo (Zosyn) — IV

  • Broad: G+, G− including Pseudomonas, anaerobes
  • Does NOT cover: MRSA, most VRE, Atypicals, ESBLs
  • PNA/Pseudomonas dosing: 4.5 g q6h (vs 3.375 g q6h for other indications)

docs.title: B. Cephalosporins
docs.text:
General Rules

  • No cephalosporin covers Enterococcus (except Ceftaroline)
  • Only Ceftazidime and Cefepime cover Pseudomonas
  • Only Cefoxitin and Cefotetan have good anaerobic coverage
Generation Drug Coverage highlights 1st Cefazolin (IV), Cephalexin (PO) MSSA, Strep, minor GN (PEK) 2nd Cefuroxime + H. flu, Neisseria 2nd (cephamycin) Cefoxitin, Cefotetan Anaerobes, GN — no Pseudo, weak GP 3rd Ceftriaxone Excellent GN, good GP — no Pseudo, no anaerobes 3rd Ceftazidime GN only including Pseudomonas — virtually no GP 4th Cefepime Broad GP + GN including Pseudomonas, weak anaerobes 5th Ceftaroline MRSA + Strep + GN (like Ceftriaxone) — no Pseudo

SPICE-A organisms

Serratia, Pseudomonas/Providencia, Indole-positive Proteus, Citrobacter, Enterobacter, Acinetobacter — have inducible AmpC beta-lactamases. Avoid lower-gen cephalosporins for serious infections. Use Cefepime, Pip/Tazo, or Carbapenem.

docs.title: C. Carbapenems
docs.text:
Drugs: Imipenem, Meropenem, Ertapenem, Doripenem (all IV)

  • Broadest spectrum — G+, G− including Pseudomonas and ESBL, anaerobes
  • Does NOT cover: MRSA, most VRE, Atypicals, Stenotrophomonas
  • Ertapenem ≠ Pseudomonas — convenient once-daily dosing, still covers ESBL

docs.title: D. Monobactam — Aztreonam
docs.text:
• Spectrum: Aerobic gram-negatives only (similar to Ceftazidime)
  • No cross-reactivity with PCN allergy ← key advantage
  • Significant Pseudomonas resistance at many institutions → empirically double-cover

docs.title: 1. Macrolides (50S)
docs.text:
• Azithromycin = DOC for most atypical infections
  • ~25% Strep pneumo resistance → combine with Ceftriaxone for hospitalized CAP
  • Side effects: QT prolongation, GI

docs.title: 2. Tetracyclines — Doxycycline (30S)
docs.text:
• Covers: Atypicals, MRSA (skin), Rickettsia, Lyme, Tularemia, Anthrax
  • DOC for early Lyme disease
  • Good choice for MRSA skin infections (but poor strep coverage → add beta-lactam)

docs.title: 3. Clindamycin (50S)
docs.text:
• Covers: Anaerobes + Gram+ cocci (Strep, Staph including ~50% CA-MRSA) — NOT Enterococcus
  • Antitoxin effect in TSS / necrotizing fasciitis (Group A Strep)
  • Always check D-test before using for MRSA
  • Does NOT penetrate CSF
  • Highest rate of C. diff (~10%) of all antibiotics

docs.title: 4. Aminoglycosides (30S)
docs.text:
• Covers: Aerobic gram-negatives including Pseudomonas — no GP, no anaerobes
  • Main use: serious GN infections + synergy for endocarditis (Enterococcal > Strep > Staph)
  • Side effects: Nephrotoxicity (reversible), ototoxicity (irreversible)
  • Concentration-dependent killing — high peak matters more than time above MIC

docs.title: III. Fluoroquinolones (DNA Gyrase Inhibitors)
docs.text:
All FQs: excellent bioavailability (use PO whenever possible), atypical coverage, QT prolongation risk

Drug Key features Ciprofloxacin Best GN coverage, virtually no GP — UTI, Pseudomonas double-coverage Levofloxacin "Respiratory FQ" — excellent Strep pneumo, CAP monotherapy ok Moxifloxacin Best anaerobes/GP — no urinary or Pseudomonas activity

TB caution

FQs cover TB (Moxi > Levo > Cipro). If you suspect TB, do NOT use FQs — monotherapy causes resistance.

docs.title: IV. TMP-SMX (Bactrim)
docs.text:
• Covers: Most CA-MRSA, PCP, Nocardia, Toxoplasma, Listeria, Stenotrophomonas, UTI pathogens
  • Best oral option for CA-MRSA skin infections (weak strep coverage → add Cephalexin)
  • ~100% PO bioavailability
  • Side effects: Rash, hyperkalemia (esp. high dose/CKD), pseudo-Cr elevation (~20%), bone marrow suppression

docs.title: Vancomycin
docs.text:
• Gold standard for MRSA
  • NOT as effective as Nafcillin for MSSA
  • PO form not absorbed → used for severe C. diff
  • Goal trough: 15–20 for serious/invasive MRSA; 20 for CNS infections
  • Avoid if MRSA MIC ≥ 2 (increased treatment failure)

docs.title: Linezolid
docs.text:
• Covers all GP including MRSA and VRE
  • 100% PO bioavailability
  • May be superior to Vancomycin for MRSA pneumonia (better lung penetration)
  • Side effects: Thrombocytopenia (check CBC weekly if >1 week use), serotonin syndrome with SSRIs, lactic acidosis (long-term)

docs.title: Daptomycin
docs.text:
• Rapidly cidal, covers MRSA and VRE
  • CANNOT use for pneumonia — inactivated by pulmonary surfactant
  • Side effect: Myopathy/rhabdomyolysis — check baseline + weekly CK, stop statins
  • Resistance commonly emerges with long-term use

docs.title: VI. Anti-Pseudomonal Options
docs.text:
For serious Pseudomonas infections → empirically double-cover until susceptibilities back:

  1. Pip/Tazo (Zosyn)
  2. Ceftazidime, Cefepime
  3. Carbapenems (not Ertapenem)
  4. Aztreonam (high resistance rates — use only if PCN-allergic)
  5. Ciprofloxacin (~70% coverage) > Levofloxacin (~65%)
  6. Aminoglycosides — do NOT use as monotherapy for serious infections

docs.title: VII. Anaerobe Coverage
docs.text:
• Above diaphragm: Clindamycin (covers Peptostreptococcus, microaerophilic Strep)
  • Below diaphragm: Metronidazole (excellent Bacteroides coverage)

Avoid for serious intraabdominal infections

Clindamycin, Moxifloxacin, Cefotetan/Cefoxitin — high Bacteroides resistance (up to 40%)

docs.title: VIII. Vanc/Zosyn — What It Misses
docs.text:
Good empiric combo, but watch for:

  1. Atypicals (Legionella, Mycoplasma, Chlamydia) — not adequate for CAP
  2. VRE — suspect if prior vancomycin exposure
  3. ESBL — suspect if recent hospitalization + broad-spectrum abx
  4. Fungal infections — esp. with TPN, central lines, immunosuppression
  5. C. difficile
  6. Stenotrophomonas — esp. with prior carbapenem use
  7. TB / Mycobacteria
  8. Viral infections (influenza, HSV, CMV)
  9. Source control — abx won't cure undrained abscess, infected line, empyema

Step up from Vanc/Zosyn

→ Linezolid/Meropenem: gains VRE and ESBL coverage, possibly better MRSA PNA coverage

docs.title: Sepsis
docs.text: Last updated: Apr 2026

docs.title: Definitions
docs.text: Term Definition Sepsis-1 ≥2 SIRS criteria + suspected infection Severe Sepsis Sepsis + organ dysfunction or tissue hypoperfusion Septic Shock (old) Hypoperfusion despite fluids OR lactate ≥4.0 mmol/L Sepsis-3 (current) Life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated host response to infection (SOFA ↑≥2) Septic Shock (Sepsis-3) Sepsis + vasopressor-dependent hypotension + lactate >2 mmol/L without hypovolemia

docs.title: SIRS Criteria
docs.text:
≥2 of the following:

Criterion Threshold Temperature >38°C or <36°C Heart Rate >90 bpm Respiratory Rate >20 breaths/min or PaCO₂ <32 mmHg WBC >12,000/µL or <4,000/µL or >10% bands

docs.title: qSOFA
docs.text:
≥2 of the following = high risk for poor outcome:

Criterion Threshold Systolic BP ≤100 mmHg Respiratory Rate ≥22 breaths/min Altered Mental Status GCS <15

docs.title: SOFA Score
docs.text:
Each category scored 0–4 (total 0–24). An increase of ≥2 from baseline = organ dysfunction.

System Parameter 1 2 3 4 Respiratory PaO₂/FiO₂ <400 <300 <200 + vent <100 + vent Coagulation Platelets (×10³/µL) <150 <100 <50 <20 Liver Bilirubin (mg/dL) 1.2–1.9 2.0–5.9 6.0–11.9 ≥12 Cardiovascular MAP or vasopressors MAP <70 Dopamine ≤5 or Dobutamine Dopamine >5 or NE/Epi ≤0.1 Dopamine >15 or NE/Epi >0.1 CNS GCS 13–14 10–12 6–9 <6 Renal Creatinine (mg/dL) or UO 1.2–1.9 2.0–3.4 3.5–4.9 or <500 mL/day ≥5 or <200 mL/day

Mortality

Initial SOFA score >11 carries ~95% mortality.

docs.title: NEWS Score (National Early Warning Score)
docs.text: Parameter 3 2 1 0 1 2 3 Resp Rate ≤8 — 9–11 12–20 — 21–24 ≥25 SpO₂ ≤91 92–93 94–95 ≥96 — — — Supplemental O₂ — Yes — No — — — Temp (°C) ≤35.0 — 35.1–36.0 36.1–38.0 38.1–39.0 ≥39.1 — Systolic BP ≤90 91–100 101–110 111–219 — — ≥220 Heart Rate ≤40 — 41–50 51–90 91–110 111–130 ≥131 Consciousness — — — Alert — — CVPU Score Risk Response 0–4 Low Routine monitoring 5–6 or any single score 3 Medium Urgent review ≥7 High Emergency response

docs.title: End-Organ Dysfunction Criteria (CMS)
docs.text:
• SBP <90 or MAP <70 mmHg
  • Creatinine >2.0 mg/dL or UOP <0.5 mL/kg/hr for >2 hours
  • Bilirubin >2 mg/dL
  • Platelets <100,000
  • Coagulopathy: INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 seconds
  • Lactate >2 mmol/L

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text: Hemodynamics Lactate Disposition Normotensive ≤2 mmol/L Floor Normotensive 2.0–3.5 mmol/L MICU consult Hypotensive OR ≥3.5 mmol/L ICU Vasopressor-dependent OR ≥4 mmol/L ICU

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Sepsis is a life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. It exists on a spectrum — from infection alone to septic shock — and carries an in-hospital mortality of ~15%, rising to 40–50% in septic shock.

Risk Factors:

  • Advanced age (>65 years)
  • Nursing home / long-term care residence
  • Comorbidities: diabetes, CHF, COPD, liver disease, cancer, CKD
  • Immunosuppression: chemotherapy, chronic steroids, immunomodulators

Common Sources:

  • Pneumonia
  • UTI
  • Central line infection
  • Skin / soft tissue infection

docs.title: Initial Actions and Primary Survey
docs.text:
High Yield

Time-sensitive — don't wait for a perfect diagnosis. Treat empirically while working up the source.

Immediate Steps:

  1. Obtain IV access (×2 large bore) and place on continuous monitoring
  2. Draw lactate (venous acceptable) — must be a fresh sample
  3. Draw 2 sets of blood cultures from 2 different sites before antibiotics
  4. Obtain source-directed cultures (UA/urine culture, sputum, wound, etc.)
  5. Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics as early as possible
  6. Begin 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid (Lactated Ringers preferred) for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
  7. Reassess frequently — patients can decompensate 30–60 minutes after antibiotics

docs.title: Presentation
docs.text:
Classic:

  • Fever, tachycardia, tachypnea, hypotension, altered mental status

Atypical (do not miss):

  • Malaise, fatigue, weakness
  • Mild delirium with inattention
  • Patient may appear well initially — especially elderly and immunocompromised

High-Risk Populations

Elderly, immunocompromised, and nursing home patients may present without fever or leukocytosis. Maintain high suspicion.

docs.title: Diagnostic Testing
docs.text:
Labs:

Test Purpose CBC with differential Leukocytosis, leukopenia, bandemia BMP / CMP Renal and hepatic dysfunction, glucose Lactate (venous) Tissue hypoperfusion, risk stratification Blood cultures ×2 Before antibiotics — identify organism Urinalysis + urine culture UTI as source Coagulation studies (INR, aPTT) DIC screening LFTs Hepatic dysfunction

Lactate Interpretation:

Level Significance ≤2 mmol/L Normal 2–4 mmol/L Elevated — repeat after resuscitation ≥4 mmol/L Severely elevated — aggressive resuscitation, ICU

Lactate represents anaerobic metabolism during inadequate oxygen delivery. Elevated levels correlate directly with mortality.

Imaging:

  • CXR (PA/lateral if stable, portable if critical) — rule out pneumonia
  • CT abdomen/pelvis if abdominal source suspected
  • Additional imaging guided by clinical presentation

docs.title: Antibiotics
docs.text:
• Start within 1 hour of recognition
  • Guide selection by suspected source and institutional antibiogram
  • Obtain cultures first, but do not delay antibiotics for cultures

docs.title: Fluid Resuscitation
docs.text:
• 30 mL/kg IV Lactated Ringers for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
  • Reassess after each fluid challenge
  • Avoid in pulmonary edema or cardiogenic shock

docs.title: Vasopressors
docs.text:
• Start if MAP remains <65 after fluids
  • First line: Norepinephrine (adults)
  • Dopamine: alternative in select cases (infants/children)

docs.title: CMS SEP-1 Bundles
docs.text:
3-Hour Bundle:

  • [ ] Measure lactate
  • [ ] Blood cultures before antibiotics
  • [ ] Broad-spectrum antibiotics
  • [ ] 30 mL/kg LR for hypotension or lactate ≥4

6-Hour Bundle:

  • [ ] Vasopressors for refractory hypotension (target MAP ≥65)
  • [ ] Reassess volume status
  • [ ] Repeat lactate if initial ≥4 mmol/L

Surviving Sepsis Hour-1 Bundle (2018):

  • Measure lactate (remeasure if elevated)
  • Blood cultures before antibiotics
  • Broad-spectrum antibiotics
  • 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4
  • Vasopressors if hypotensive during or after resuscitation

Controversy

The Hour-1 Bundle was initially rejected by ACEP and SCCM. ProCESS, ARISE, and ProMISe trials found no mortality difference between EGDT, standardized care, and routine care. CVP and ScvO₂ monitoring are no longer emphasized.

docs.title: Pearls and Pitfalls
docs.text:
Pearls

  • Maintain high suspicion in elderly, immunocompromised, and nursing home patients
  • A fresh lactate sample is essential — do not use old draws
  • Blood cultures must be from two separate sites before antibiotics
  • Reassess frequently — decompensation can happen 30–60 min after initial treatment
  • Use your institution's screening tools and order sets
  • Lactate clearance (targeting ≤2 mmol/L or 10% reduction) is non-inferior to EGDT

Pitfalls

  • Anchoring on a "benign" presentation — sepsis can look subtle
  • Delaying antibiotics waiting for cultures or imaging
  • Giving 30 mL/kg fluids to a patient with pulmonary edema or cardiogenic shock
  • Missing atypical presentations in elderly patients (no fever, normal WBC)
  • Not repeating lactate after resuscitation to confirm clearance

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Singer M, et al. The Third International Consensus Definitions for Sepsis and Septic Shock (Sepsis-3). JAMA. 2016;315(8):801–810.
  2. Kumar A, et al. Duration of hypotension before initiation of effective antimicrobial therapy is the critical determinant of survival in human septic shock. Crit Care Med. 2006;34(6):1589–1596.
  3. ProCESS Investigators. A randomized trial of protocol-based care for early septic shock. NEJM. 2014;370:1683–1693.
  4. ARISE Investigators. Goal-directed resuscitation for patients with early septic shock. NEJM. 2014;371:1496–1506.
  5. Mouncey PR, et al. (ProMISe). Trial of early, goal-directed resuscitation for septic shock. NEJM. 2015;372:1301–1311.
  6. Schneider D, Burnsides C, Gustafson L, Engineer R. Sepsis. SAEM CDEM M4 Curriculum. Updated November 2019.
  7. Rhodes A, et al. Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2016. Crit Care Med. 2017;45(3):486–552.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Shock
  • Clinical Overview

docs.title: Shock
docs.text: Last updated: Mar 21, 2026

docs.title: Shock Definition
docs.text:
• Definition: Inadequate Tissue Perfusion to Meet End-Organ Needs
  • Cellular/Tissue Hypoxia from Reduced Oxygen Delivery, Increased Oxygen Consumption, or Inadequate Oxygen Utilization
  • Generally Caused by Circulatory Failure with Hypotension
    • Not Based Solely on Vital Signs
    • Can Present with Hypertension in Certain Cases

In other words: VO₂ (oxygen consumption) > DO₂ (oxygen delivery)

docs.title: DO₂ / VO₂ Curve
docs.text:
Shock State (left of Critical DO₂) Normal State (right of Critical DO₂) Cellular VO₂ is dependent on DO₂. Cells would consume more oxygen, but there is no more being delivered. These cells depend on anaerobic metabolism and are producing lactate and will eventually die. Cellular VO₂ is independent of DO₂. These cells depend on aerobic metabolism and are not producing lactate. Moving leftward on this curve represents either an increase in cellular VO₂ or a decrease in DO₂. 
        "Critical DO₂"
              ↓
VO₂  |       /‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾
     |      /
     |     /
     |    /
     |___/
              DO₂

docs.title: Core Formula
docs.text: VO₂ > CO × CaO₂

docs.title: Shock Classifications
docs.text:
Distributive Shock — Severe Peripheral Vasodilation (50% — Most Common)

  • Septic Shock
  • Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (SIRS)
  • Neurogenic Shock
  • Anaphylactic Shock
  • Endocrine Shock (Addisonian/Adrenal Crisis)
  • Drug/Toxin-Induced Shock

Cardiogenic Shock — Cardiac Etiology Causing Reduced Cardiac Output (14%)

Hypovolemic Shock — From Reduced Intravascular Volume (31%)

  • Hemorrhagic Shock
  • Non-Hemorrhagic Hypovolemic Shock

Obstructive Shock — Extra-Cardiac Etiology of Cardiac Pump Failure (1%)

docs.title: Mortality
docs.text: Type Mortality Distributive Shock 20–50% — Septic Shock 40–50% — Neurogenic Shock 13–23% — Endocrine Shock (Adrenal Crisis) 6% Cardiogenic Shock 50–75% (Highest Risk) Hypovolemic Shock 20–37%

docs.title: Types of Shock — Hemodynamic Profile
docs.text: Type Examples Preload Afterload Contractility Hypovolemic Dehydration, hemorrhage ↓↓ ↑ ↑ Distributive Anaphylaxis, neurogenic ↓ ↓ ↑ Cardiogenic CHD, myocarditis, arrhythmia, MI ↑ ↑ ↓↓ Obstructive Tamponade, pneumothorax, PE ↓ ↑↑ ↑ Dissociative Anemia, hypothyroid, CO toxicity ↕ ↕ ↑

docs.title: Workup and Monitoring
docs.text: (Notes to be added)

docs.title: Treatment Principles
docs.text:
The treatment of shock has three objectives:

  1. Increase DO₂
  2. Decrease VO₂
  3. Treat the underlying cause

docs.title: Full Treatment Framework
docs.text:
VO₂ > (HR × SV) × (1.34 × Hgb × SaO₂ + 0.003 × PaO₂)

(where HR × SV = CO, and 1.34 × Hgb × SaO₂ + 0.003 × PaO₂ = CaO₂)

Decrease VO₂ (O₂ Consumption):

  • Sedation
  • Analgesia
  • Paralysis
  • PPV
  • Treat seizures
  • Treat arrhythmias
  • Treat fever

Increase CO (HR × SV):

Component Intervention HR Increase chronotropy, increase dromotropy, treat arrhythmias Preload (EDV) Vasoconstrictors, intravascular bolus Afterload (ESV) Vasodilators, PPV Contractility Inotropic agents, temperature, normal pH, normal electrolytes, remove toxins

Increase CaO₂:

  • pRBC transfusion
  • Increase FiO₂, adequate ventilation
  • Maintain PEEP, optimize Qp:Qs
  • Avoid alteration in Hgb O₂ offloading

docs.title: Vasopressors and Vasoactives
docs.text:
BP = CO × SVR = (HR × SV) × SVR

Receptor β₁ D₁, D₂, D₄, D₅ α₁ V₁ β₂ Others Location Heart Heart, Blood vessels Blood vessels Blood vessels Blood vessels — Effects Inotropy, Chronotropy, Dromotropy Chronotropy, Inotropy Vasoconstriction Vasoconstriction Vasodilation Milrinone: Inotropy + Vasodilation; Nitroprusside: Vasodilation Drugs Isoproterenol, Dobutamine, Epinephrine, Dopamine (high dose) Dopamine Phenylephrine, Norepinephrine, Epinephrine (high dose), Dopamine (high dose) Vasopressin Isoproterenol, Dobutamine, Epinephrine (low dose) —

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • Clinical Overview

docs.title: 2026 Sepsis Guidelines
docs.text: Reference: traumaicu.org/sepsis

docs.title: Panel
docs.text:
• Most diverse to date: 68 people from 23 countries
  • 6 different subgroups:
    • Screening and early management
    • Infection
    • Thermodynamics
    • Respiratory support
    • Adjunctive therapies
    • Goals of care and outcomes
  • Methods: Standard practices; identify and prioritize questions in guidelines (PICO format)

docs.title: Screening and Early Management
docs.text:
• Rec 9: Sepsis is an emergency and treatment should begin immediately
  • Rec 3: For acutely ill adults en route to hospital by ambulance or flight, suggest using a standard sepsis screening tool over not using one
    • Speeds time to dx and overall treatment
  • Rec 21: For adults with definite or probable sepsis and hypotension (septic shock) with anticipated time to hospital evaluation >60 min, suggest administering antibiotics in ambulance or flight
    • Prehospital abx should only be delivered after having established protocol for sepsis screening (as above)
  • Rec 2: For hospitals and health systems, suggest using a "code sepsis" or "sepsis huddle" protocol
    • Screening process in place that flags patients as having potential sepsis
    • RRT or provider then evaluates if patient has sepsis and begins workup
  • Rec 10: For adults with sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock, suggest administering at least 30 mL/kg of IV crystalloid in the first 3 hours
    • Frequent reassessments to avoid harm
    • Calculate based on actual body weight for normal/underweight patients
    • Calculate based on ideal body weight for obese patients (BMI >30)

docs.title: Infection
docs.text:
ABX Timing: Same as 2021 guidelines

ABX Selection:

  • For adults with sepsis or septic shock: suggest against empirical antifungal therapy
    • Consider only if: immunocompromised, prolonged use of antibiotics or hospitalization, or intra-abdominal infection
  • Recommend using prolonged infusion of beta-lactams for maintenance (after initial loading dose) over bolus administration — very strong recommendation

De-escalation:

  • Evaluate for specific anatomical diagnosis or sources of infection that require emergent source control
  • If source control needed: ideally within 6 hours (low certainty evidence)

docs.title: Hemodynamics
docs.text:
• First-line vasopressor: IV Epinephrine
  • Target MAP >65
  • Use invasive or non-invasive dynamic measures to guide initial fluid resuscitation over PE or static measures alone
    • Dynamic measures:
      • Passive leg raise
      • Pulse pressure variation

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Sepsis
  • ICU Notes Overview

docs.title: Agitation
docs.text: The Agitated Patient

docs.title: Interview Safety
docs.text:
Safety First

Safety of staff is paramount. Call security if necessary.

  • Patients should always be disarmed before any interaction
  • No potentially dangerous objects should be available
  • Do not let the patient sit between the physician and the exit

docs.title: Risk Factors for Violence
docs.text:
• History of violence
  • Male gender
  • Alcohol / drug abuse

docs.title: Signs of Impending Violence
docs.text:
Provocative Behavior:

  • Angry demeanor
  • Loud, aggressive speech
  • Tense posturing
  • Frequently changing body position / pacing
  • Aggressive acts (pounding wall, throwing objects)
  • Verbal threats

docs.title: Assess for Medical Causes that May Be Contributing
docs.text:
• Hypoxia
  • Inadequate pain control
  • Hypoglycemia
  • Infection
  • Drug overdose
  • Intracranial hemorrhage

docs.title: General Approach
docs.text:
• Manage delirium as noted — see Delirium
  • Initial nonpharmacologic attempts are preferred, unless violent or a danger to self/others
  • Immediate sedation: start with a rapid-onset antipsychotic or benzodiazepine
    • Avoid opioids and benzodiazepines in the setting of delirium when possible — can worsen delirium
    • First-generation (typical) antipsychotics have risk for QT prolongation — monitor ECG
  • Continued sedation: may be required once safety is achieved
  • Consider physical restraints if an imminent harm to self or others — should be considered a last resort; may also worsen delirium

docs.title: Nonpharmacologic Techniques
docs.text:
• Frequent reorientation and reassurance
  • Listen to the patient — be attentive and receptive
  • Be honest and straightforward
  • Avoid arguing or condescending
  • Respect personal space
  • Use concise and simple language
  • Offer a chair
  • Use of a bedside sitter

docs.title: Immediate Sedation
docs.text:
First Generation (Typical) Antipsychotics:

Drug Dose Notes Droperidol 2.5–5 mg IM/IV More rapid onset, greater efficacy over haloperidol for acute psychosis/agitation, fewer EPS Haloperidol (Haldol) 2–5 mg IM/IV

Benzodiazepines:

Drug Dose Notes Midazolam (Versed) 2.5–5 mg IM/IV More rapid onset but shorter duration than lorazepam Lorazepam (Ativan) 2–4 mg IM/IV

Combination Therapy

Both antipsychotics and benzodiazepines together achieve more rapid sedation.

Ketamine:

  • Bolus: 1–2 mg/kg over 30–60 seconds
  • Repeat: 0.5–1.0 mg/kg after 5–10 minutes if needed

docs.title: Continued Sedation
docs.text:
Antipsychotic Medications:

  • Quetiapine (Seroquel)
  • Valproic Acid (Depakote)
  • Olanzapine (Zyprexa)
  • Risperidone (Risperdal)
  • Haloperidol (Haldol)

Continuous IV Sedation:

  • Dexmedetomidine (Precedex)
  • Ketamine

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Delirium
  • ICU Notes Overview

docs.title: Breathing Support
docs.text: Oxygen Delivery, Consumption, and Extraction Ratio

docs.title: Definitions
docs.text: Term Meaning DO₂ Oxygen delivery VO₂ Oxygen consumption O₂ER Oxygen extraction ratio CaO₂ Arterial O₂ content CvO₂ Venous oxygen content CO Cardiac output

docs.title: Oxygen Delivery (DO₂)
docs.text: DO₂ = CaO₂ × CO

docs.title: Cardiac Output (CO)
docs.text:
CO = HR × SV

Factors that Increase Stroke Volume:

  • Increased preload (end-diastolic volume)
  • Increased contractility
  • Decreased afterload / decreased SVR

docs.title: Arterial Oxygen Content (CaO₂)
docs.text:
Arterial oxygen content is a function of three variables:

Variable Source Notes Hemoglobin (Hgb) CBC Most clinically significant determinant of CaO₂ SaO₂ Pulse ox (SpO₂) Same impact as Hgb, but generally less variance PaO₂ ABG Least important — only contributes 1–2%

Oxygen carrying capacity of Hgb = amount of O₂ (in mL) bound per gram of Hgb = constant 1.34

Oxygen partially dissolves in plasma, calculated as: 0.003 × PaO₂

CaO₂ = (Hgb × SaO₂ × 1.34) + (PaO₂ × 0.003)

DO₂ = CO × (Hgb × SaO₂ × 1.34 + PaO₂ × 0.003)

docs.title: Oxy-Hgb Dissociation Curve
docs.text:
Compares partial pressure of oxygen (PaO₂) to percent oxygen saturation of hemoglobin (SaO₂).

Shift Effect Causes Left shift ↑ Affinity → O₂ binding ↓ CO₂, ↓ Temp, ↓ H⁺ (↑ pH), ↓ 2,3-DPG, CO exposure, high altitude Right shift ↓ Affinity → O₂ unloading ↑ CO₂, ↑ Temp, ↑ H⁺ (↓ pH), ↑ 2,3-DPG, hypoxia

Special Notes:

  • Carbon monoxide increases O₂ affinity at other binding sites → left shift
  • Hypoxia further decreases O₂ affinity due to 2,3-DPG having increased affinity for deoxygenated Hgb
  • Fetal Hgb has low affinity for 2,3-DPG → overall higher affinity for O₂

docs.title: Arterial Blood Gas (ABG)
docs.text:
• Measures and reports PaO₂ (used in P:F ratio, etc.)
  • May also measure SaO₂

ABG Challenges:

  • Expensive, painful, and time consuming
  • Blood loss
  • May be contaminated with venous blood
  • Although a better measure of lung function, pulse ox better measures systemic delivery
  • Often misinterpreted — SpO₂ of 88% correlates with PaO₂ of ~55 mmHg

docs.title: Pulse Oximeter (Pulse Ox)
docs.text:
• Detects SpO₂ — aspect of respiration, not ventilation

Pulse Ox Challenges:

  • May have a bias of +2% in dark skin — oxygen saturation may be overestimated by ~2%, leading to increased rates of unrecognized hypoxemia
  • Requires pulse detection in peripheral circulation — may be abnormal in shock or hypothermia
  • Generally not accurate once below 70%
  • Falsely elevated in carbon monoxide poisoning

docs.title: ABG vs. Pulse Ox
docs.text:
Pulse oximetry (SpO₂) is generally superior to ABG (PaO₂) in measuring oxygenation.

When ABG is preferred:

  • If pulse oximeter waveform is unreliable (see "plethysmographic waveform")
  • For P/F ratio calculation in ARDS
  • Diagnosis of methemoglobinemia

docs.title: Venous Blood Gas (VBG)
docs.text:
Sources:

  • Peripheral — from peripheral veins
  • Central venous — from a central line
  • Mixed venous — from a pulmonary artery catheter

Benefits over ABG:

  • Easier to obtain, less painful
  • Can be obtained while sampling other labs
  • Lower risk of complications (hematoma, dissection, thrombosis)

Challenges:

  • ABG is still the gold standard for evaluating oxygenation, ventilation, and acid-base status
  • VBG is generally less well validated than ABG
  • VBG and ABG may have worse agreement in severe circulatory failure / hypotension
  • VBG may correlate better for pH and pCO₂ than for PaO₂ or SaO₂

docs.title: General Target Values
docs.text: Parameter Standard COPD / ARDS SaO₂ / SpO₂ >92% 88% PaO₂ >60 mmHg 55 mmHg ScvO₂ >70% —

docs.title: Hypoxic Definitions
docs.text:
• Hypoxemia: Low oxygen content in blood
  • Hypoxia: Low oxygen content in tissues

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• NIPPV
  • Ventilators
  • ICU Notes Overview

docs.title: Definitions
docs.text:
Delirium (DSM-5 Characteristics):

  • Disturbance in awareness and attention
    • Reduced ability to direct, focus, sustain, and shift attention
  • Develops over a short period of time (hours–days), represents a change from baseline, and tends to fluctuate throughout the day
  • An additional disturbance in cognition is present
    • Memory, disorientation, language, visuospatial ability, or perception
  • Caused by: medical condition, intoxication, substance withdrawal, or medication side effects
  • Not better explained by another neurocognitive disorder

Subtypes:

Subtype Features Hypoactive Somnolence and psychomotor retardation Hyperactive Agitation and increased psychomotor activity Mixed Fluctuations between the two

Special Contexts:

Term Definition Postoperative Delirium Delirium that presents in the post-op period Emergence Delirium Delirium in the OR immediately after extubation ICU Delirium Delirium that presents during an ICU stay Sundowning Behavioral deterioration during evening hours; most common with preexisting dementia

docs.title: Risk Factors
docs.text:
Patient Factors:

  • Elderly
  • Preexisting dementia — strongest RF in the elderly
  • Malnutrition
  • ETOH abuse
  • Medical comorbidities
  • Psych meds
  • Type of surgery: 35% after vascular surgery; 40–60% after hip replacement

Precipitating Factors:

  • Severe illness
  • Infection
  • Medications
  • Hypoxia
  • Electrolyte abnormalities
  • Pain
  • Dehydration

ICU-Specific Factors:

  • Bladder catheter use
  • Sensory or functional impairment
  • Physical restraints
  • Sleep deprivation
  • Unfamiliar environment

docs.title: Features and Complications
docs.text:
Common Associated Features:

  • Psychomotor behavioral disturbances: hypoactivity or hyperactivity with increased sympathetic activity
  • Sleep impairment
  • Variable emotional disturbances: fear, depression, euphoria, perplexity

Complications:

  • Delirium-induced persistent cognitive dysfunction can last months–years
  • Can accelerate cognitive decline of existing dementia
  • Increased morbidity and mortality:
Timeline Mortality 1-month after single-day delirium 14.5% 1-month after ≥3 days of delirium 39% 6-month mortality Increased 3.2-fold 12-month mortality Increases 10% per day of delirium
  • Increased length of stay
  • Increased rate of discharge to skilled nursing facility
  • Increased rate of readmission
  • Increased cost

docs.title: CAM-ICU (Confusion Assessment Method for ICU)
docs.text:
Initial evaluation to assess for delirium. High sensitivity and specificity. Can be used in ventilated patients.

Features:

  1. Acute onset and fluctuating course
  2. Inattention
  3. Disorganized thinking
  4. Altered level of consciousness

Delirium = Features 1 AND 2 + Either 3 OR 4

For evaluation of the agitated patient, see Agitation.

docs.title: Prevention and Treatment
docs.text:
Main Principles:

  • Treat underlying medical conditions
  • Appropriate pain control with non-opioid medications
  • Avoid opioids and benzodiazepines
  • Frequent reorientation
  • Allow regular visits from family and friends for cognitive stimulation
  • Support physiologic sleep patterns:
    • Avoid unnecessary nighttime interruptions and reduce nighttime noises
    • Avoid excessive daytime sleeping
    • Provide clocks and keep lights on with windows open during the day
  • Avoid physical restraints — can worsen delirium
  • Early mobilization with physical therapy
  • Ensure vision and hearing aids are accessible
  • May require use of a bedside sitter

Management of Agitation:

  • Initial nonpharmacologic attempts are preferred
  • May require sedation if violent or a danger to self/others
  • See Agitation

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Agitation
  • ICU Notes Overview

docs.title: ICP
docs.text: Intracranial Pressure

docs.title: Cerebral Edema
docs.text:
• No evidence that hypertonic solution helps lower ICP in severe metabolic encephalopathy

There are four main pathologies for ICP. Differentiating between the two most common causes has an important clinical distinction regarding corticosteroid use:

Type Cause Corticosteroids Vasogenic (inflammatory) Brain tumors, inflammatory conditions involving BBB disruption ✅ Indicated Cytotoxic Infarcts (dead tissue) → malignant cerebral edema; ischemic stroke, ICH, TBI ❌ Contraindicated

Corticosteroid Pearl

CCS are indicated for vasogenic edema (brain tumors, BBB disruption). CCS are contraindicated in cytotoxic edema (ischemic stroke, ICH, TBI).

docs.title: Renal Replacement Therapy
docs.text: • CRRT is more gentle than HD for patients with cerebral edema

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• TBI
  • ICU Notes Overview

docs.title: ICU GI Prophylaxis
docs.text:
Stress Ulcer Prophylaxis — SCCM Guidelines 2024

Reference: SCCM/ASHP Guideline: Prevention of UGIB

docs.title: Big 3 Indications for Critically Ill Adults
docs.text:
1. Shock
  2. Chronic liver disease
  3. Coagulopathy

docs.title: Mechanical Ventilation Controversy
docs.text:
Previously, standard practice was to give SUP to anyone on mechanical ventilation without parenteral feeding.

Most recent guidelines had dropped this recommendation. However, the REVISE trial indicated decreased GI bleed with prophylaxis in mechanically ventilated patients.

REVISE Trial: Stress ulcer prophylaxis during invasive mechanical ventilation. NEJM 2024.

Clear as mud, right?

The mechanical ventilation indication remains debated. Follow your institution's protocol.

docs.title: ICU Presentation
docs.text: Template for ICU patient presentations

docs.title: Presentation Template
docs.text:
1. Major overnight events
  2. Drips currently on
  3. Vent settings (if applicable)
  4. Review by system — start with patient presentation and physical exam
  5. State recommendations

docs.title: PEARLS by System
docs.text: (Notes to be added by system)

docs.title: IV Contrast AKI
docs.text:
Bottom Line

The risk of contrast-associated AKI has been grossly overstated.

docs.title: Provider Note
docs.text: It is medically necessary for the patient to receive the requested contrasted scan despite poor kidney function. The benefits outweigh the risks. As appropriate, the patient will be appropriately hydrated and/or treated pre/post imaging with the appropriate labs trended.

docs.title: ACR / NKF Consensus Statement
docs.text:
Reference: Davenport MS, et al. "Use of Intravenous Iodinated Contrast Media in Patients with Kidney Disease: Consensus Statements from the American College of Radiology and the National Kidney Foundation." Radiology 294.3 (2020): 660–668.

Summary:

The putative risk of administering modern intravenous iodinated contrast media in patients with reduced kidney function has been overstated — primarily because of conflation of contrast-associated AKI (CA-AKI) with contrast-induced AKI (CI-AKI) in uncontrolled studies.

Key Points:

  • True risk of CI-AKI remains unknown
  • Prophylaxis with IV normal saline is indicated (without contraindication, e.g. heart failure) for:
    • Patients with AKI, or
    • eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73 m² not on maintenance dialysis
  • Prophylaxis may be considered for eGFR 30–44 mL/min/1.73 m² at clinician discretion
  • Solitary kidney should not independently influence decision making
  • Do not ad hoc lower contrast dose below diagnostic threshold — risks lowering diagnostic accuracy
  • Withhold nephrotoxic medications when feasible in high-risk patients
  • Renal replacement therapy should not be initiated or altered solely based on contrast administration

docs.title: Key Supporting Articles
docs.text:
1. Aycock RD, Westafer LM, Boxen JL et al. Acute Kidney Injury after CT: A meta-analysis. Annals of Emergency Medicine 2018. (PMID 28811122)

    • Exhaustive, modern literature review showing no difference in AKI, mortality, or dialysis with contrast.

  2. Hinson JS, Jalbout NA, Ehmann MR et al. Acute kidney injury following contrast media administration in the septic patient: A retrospective propensity-matched analysis. Journal of Critical Care 2019. (PMID 28131489)

    • Large study at Johns Hopkins in patients with suspected sepsis. No effect detected from contrast dye, even among patients with GFR <30 mL/min.

  3. Ehrmann S, Aronson D, Hinson JS. Contrast-associated acute kidney injury is a myth: Yes. Intensive Care Medicine 2017. (PMID 29242967)

docs.title: Ascending (Sensory) Tracts
docs.text:
Spinothalamic Tract

  • Function: Pain and temperature sensation (contralateral)
  • First order neuron → synapse in dorsal horn → second-order neuron crosses midline and ascends

Dorsal Column (Medial Lemniscal Pathway)

  • Function: Fine touch, vibration, proprioception (ipsilateral until medulla)
  • First order neuron ascends ipsilaterally → synapses in medulla → second-order neuron crosses and ascends to thalamus

Other Ascending Tracts:

  • Spinocerebellar
  • Spinoolivary

docs.title: Descending (Motor) Tracts
docs.text:
Corticospinal Tract (Pyramidal Tract)

  • Function: Voluntary movement of contralateral side
  • First order neuron (UMN): precentral gyrus, descends ipsilaterally via internal capsule → synapses in spinal cord
  • Second-order neuron (LMN): exits to effector muscle

Extrapyramidal Tracts

Commonalities: involuntary movement, muscle tone, facial expression muscle control

Tract Origin Function Rubrospinal Red nucleus of the midbrain Motor control Reticulospinal Reticular formation (pons and medulla) Facilitation of voluntary and reflex responses, muscle tone, posture Vestibulospinal Vestibular nuclei Lateral: muscle tone in leg extensors, posture; Medial: neck musculature, head movements Tectospinal Brainstem Reflex posturing of head in response to auditory or visual stimuli

docs.title: Reflex Mnemonic
docs.text:
Remember Your Reflexes

S1–S2 — Buckle my shoe (ankle reflex, gastroc)

L2–L4 — Kick the door (knee reflex, quadriceps)

C5–C6 — Pick up sticks (biceps and brachioradialis reflexes)

C7–C8 — Lay them straight (triceps reflex)

L1–L2 — Testicle move (cremasteric reflex)

S3–S5 — Winking drive (anal wink reflex)

docs.title: Cranial Nerve Anatomy and Clinical Importance
docs.text:
CN Name Notes CN III Oculomotor Pupillary constriction, most EOMs, eyelid elevation CN VI Abducens Lateral eye movement

(Additional CN content to be added)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• TBI
  • ICP

docs.title: TBI
docs.text: Traumatic Brain Injury

docs.title: Epidural Hematoma (EDH)
docs.text:
• Definition: Bleeding between the dura mater and skull
  • Most common source: Middle meningeal artery
  • Often associated with temporal bone fracture
  • CT appearance: Lentiform / biconvex; does not cross midline

Pearl

Associated with "lucid interval" — initial LOC followed by a period of consciousness, then subsequent coma.

docs.title: Subdural Hematoma (SDH)
docs.text:
• Definition: Bleeding between the dura mater and arachnoid mater
  • Most common intracranial hemorrhage
  • Source: Venous plexus / bridging veins; acceleration/deceleration injury
  • CT appearance: Crescent-shaped; crosses midline

Pearl

  • Chronic SDH is common in elderly after falls
  • Classic mechanism: acceleration / deceleration injury

docs.title: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH)
docs.text:
• Definition: Bleeding between the brain and arachnoid mater
  • Causes:
    • Trauma (by far #1)
    • Ruptured aneurysm
    • AVM

Pearls

  • Aneurysmal bleeds = "thunderclap" headache, "worst headache of my life"
  • Xanthochromia of CSF is pathognomonic

docs.title: Diffuse Axonal Injury (DAI)
docs.text:
• Definition: Diffuse shearing injury of the brain axons
  • Most common cause: Rotational force of acceleration/deceleration impact
  • May not be apparent on CT imaging (low diagnostic yield)
  • MRI appearance: Punctate hemorrhages; blurring of grey-white interface
  • Often identified when CT is insignificant on presentation but patient fails to improve after 6–24 hours → prompts MRI

Adams Classification:

Grade Description Grade 1 Mild — microscopic white matter changes in cerebral cortex, corpus callosum, and brainstem Grade 2 Moderate — gross focal lesions in the corpus callosum Grade 3 Severe — findings of Grade 1 + additional focal lesions in the brainstem

Warning

Generally a poor prognosis.

docs.title: Intracerebral Hemorrhage (ICH)
docs.text:
• Definition: Bleeding within the brain parenchyma
  • Most common cause: HTN — chronic HTN weakens walls of small vessels, making them prone to rupture
  • Most common spontaneous HTN areas: Cerebellum, thalamus, putamen, and pons
  • Other causes:
    • Aneurysm rupture
    • Cerebral amyloid angiopathy
    • CVM
    • Coagulopathies or anticoagulant medications (least common)
